seo.title: Chumphon Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Everything about Chumphon Province: gateway to Koh Tao, Thung Wua Laen Beach, Mu Ko Chumphon National Park, and fresh seafood. Area: 6,009 km², population: 511,000. Plan your visit.

overview: Chumphon is a southern province of Thailand sitting at the top of the Malay Peninsula, on the Gulf of Thailand coast. Often called the 'Gateway to the South,' it marks the transition point where Thailand's culture, cuisine, and landscape shift from the central plains into southern Thai character. With a 222-kilometre coastline and 44 offshore islands, the province is best known as the ferry departure point for Koh Tao — one of the world's top scuba diving destinations — as well as for its pristine, uncrowded beaches, rich seafood, and thriving coffee and durian farming.
geography: Chumphon covers 6,009 km² on the narrow Isthmus of Kra, where Thailand is at its thinnest between the Gulf of Thailand and the Andaman Sea. The terrain combines coastal plains, forested hills, and river valleys. The Chumphon Archipelago comprises 44 islands, many within Mu Ko Chumphon National Park. The province borders Myanmar's Tanintharyi Region to the northwest, making it a crossroads of cultures. Approximately 21.5% of the land is forested, and the province contains four wildlife sanctuaries.
highlights: Primary ferry gateway to Koh Tao — Thailand's top scuba diving island, just 90 minutes by high-speed ferry
Thung Wua Laen Beach — a 2 km arc of white sand and turquoise water, the province's most scenic and uncrowded beach
Mu Ko Chumphon National Park — archipelago of 40 islands with coral reefs, whale sharks, and pristine diving (Feb–Oct)
Chumphon grows 60% of all Thailand's coffee — sample fresh local brews at roadside cafes throughout the province
Second-largest durian-growing region in Thailand — Mon Thong and Chanee varieties sold fresh at roadside stalls (Apr–Jun)
Prince of Chumphon Shrine — memorial to the 'Father of the Royal Thai Navy,' a highly revered figure in southern Thailand
Khao Matsi Viewpoint — hilltop 360-degree panorama with a giant Guanyin statue overlooking sea and jungle
Annual eagle migration (Sep–Oct): thousands of Siberian eagles pass over the cape in one of Asia's great wildlife spectacles

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Dec–Feb: Cooler and drier with temperatures of 24–32°C. The best months for beach visits and snorkeling. Calm Gulf of Thailand waters allow reliable ferry services to Koh Tao.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: Temperatures rise to 34–38°C but crowds remain low. Durian and fruit season peaks April–June. Diving conditions remain excellent. Songkran (April) is celebrated with water fights throughout town.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Nov: Southwest monsoon brings heavier rain and rougher Gulf seas from roughly June to October. Ferry services to Koh Tao may be disrupted. September–October sees the spectacular eagle migration. Hotel prices drop 20–30%.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit December to February for ideal beach and diving conditions. If you want durian season and fewer tourists, April–May is excellent. Avoid long ferry crossings during heavy monsoon months (Aug–Oct).

topAttractions.name: Thung Wua Laen Beach (Hat Thung Wua Laen)
topAttractions.description: Widely considered Chumphon's most beautiful beach — a 2 km sweep of powdery white sand and clear turquoise water, 12 km northeast of the provincial capital. The beach is backed by a relaxed strip of restaurants and small resorts. Activities include paddleboarding, kayaking, surfing, and boat trips to nearby islands. Refreshingly uncrowded compared to Koh Samui or Hua Hin.

topAttractions.name: Mu Ko Chumphon National Park
topAttractions.description: A marine national park covering 40 islands off the Chumphon coast, protecting some of the Gulf of Thailand's finest coral reefs. Home to whale sharks, black-tip reef sharks, colorful corals, sea anemones, and abundant reef fish. Snorkeling and diving tours depart from the mainland between February and October when seas are calmest. The HTMS Prab — a decommissioned warship sunk in 2011 — has become one of the best artificial reef dive sites in Thailand.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB foreigners / 40 THB Thais (adults); 100 THB / 20 THB (children)

sources.sourceName: Wikipedia — Mu Ko Chumphon National Park

topAttractions.name: Prince of Chumphon Shrine (Krom Luang Chumphon Khet Udomsak)
topAttractions.description: A venerated memorial to Admiral Prince Abhakara Kiartivongse, Krom Luang Chumphon Khet Udomsak — the 'Father of the Royal Thai Navy' and 28th son of King Rama V. The shrine is one of the most deeply respected in southern Thailand, attracting both pilgrims and tourists. The adjacent wharf displays the historic royal battleship. Particularly crowded during the annual festival in March.

topAttractions.name: Khao Matsi Viewpoint
topAttractions.description: A hilltop viewpoint approximately 15 km from Chumphon town offering a 360-degree panoramic view of the coastline, islands, and forested hills. The summit features a large statue of Guanyin (the Goddess of Mercy) gazing out to sea, a coffee shop, a bird's nest snack stall, and a wooden observation platform. Accessible by car or motorbike; the winding road up is part of the experience. Open daily 6 AM–9 PM.

topAttractions.name: Yai I Cave (Tham Yai I)
topAttractions.description: A scenic coastal cave located 46 km north of Chumphon town, directly above a beach so that daylight filters dramatically through the cave opening. Inside is a revered golden Buddha statue. The walk to and from the cave offers views over the Gulf of Thailand. A quiet, seldom-visited spot that rewards the effort of getting there.

topAttractions.name: Koh Phitak Island
topAttractions.description: A small island 80 km south of Chumphon town, accessible by longtail boat, where an authentic Thai fishing community still lives largely without roads or tourism infrastructure. Day trips offer a rare glimpse into traditional coastal Thai life — residents earn their living from crab traps and fishing nets. Limited homestays available for those who want to stay overnight. A genuine off-the-beaten-path experience.

localFood: Pu Ma (blue crab) — freshly steamed swimming crab eaten whole with spicy lime-chilli dipping sauce; Chumphon is famous for plump, sweet blue crabs caught just offshore (250–450 THB/kg)

Dim Sum (Tiao) — Chumphon's most celebrated breakfast tradition, especially at Hor Jiah restaurant in town; steamed dumplings and buns served from early morning with Chinese tea, a legacy of the province's Hokkien community
Gaeng Neua (spicy southern beef curry) — rich, fiery curry with deep-dried chillies and turmeric, a southern Thai staple distinct from central Thai curries
Massaman Gai — slow-cooked chicken massaman curry with potato and peanuts; a southern-Muslim influenced dish common in Chumphon's halal restaurants
Gaeng Som Tai (southern orange curry) — sour, spicy fish curry with tamarind and wild vegetables; sharper and more intense than central Thai gaeng som
Fresh Gulf seafood — whole grilled fish, giant prawns, and shellfish served at beachside restaurants along Thung Wua Laen; low tide exposes shellfish-rich tidal flats
Chumphon coffee — locally grown Robusta and Arabica from the province's highlands (60% of Thailand's total coffee output); sold freshly brewed at roadside stalls and the Chumphon coffee market
Mon Thong durian — prized premium durian grown in the hills inland; peak season April–June; sold at roadside stalls with the distinctive yellow flesh and creamy-bitter flavor
culture: Chumphon sits at the cultural frontier where central Thai traditions give way to the more distinct southern Thai identity. The province has a significant Chinese-Thai (Hokkien) community, visible in the dim sum breakfast culture, Chinese shrines, and Lunar New Year celebrations. The Prince of Chumphon Shrine is one of the most sacred religious sites in the south, drawing pilgrims from across Thailand each March. The annual Chumphon Marine Festival celebrates the province's seafaring heritage with boat races, seafood markets, and cultural performances. Local festivals include the traditional ghost festival (Pee Ta Khon-style processions) and agricultural fairs showcasing the province's famous coffee and durian harvests.
gettingThere: Chumphon is accessible by multiple modes: AirAsia and Nok Air operate flights to Chumphon Airport (CJM), located 35 km north of town, with connections from Bangkok Don Mueang (fares from ~800 THB; minibus into town ~150 THB). By train, services from Bangkok Hua Lamphong depart daily with the journey taking 7–10 hours (2nd class seats 200–600 THB, sleeper berths 400–1,450 THB). Buses from Bangkok Southern Bus Terminal take approximately 7 hours (400–700 THB). Chumphon is the main gateway for ferries to Koh Tao: Lomprayah high-speed catamaran departs twice daily (07:00 and 13:30) and takes 1 hour 45 minutes (~600 THB); Songserm Express also operates a morning service.
gettingAround: Public transport within Chumphon Province is limited and a rental motorbike (200–350 THB/day) or car hire (1,000–1,500 THB/day) is the most practical option for reaching beaches and attractions. Songthaews (shared pick-up trucks) run fixed routes between the town centre and Thung Wua Laen Beach (30–40 THB). Tuk-tuks and motorcycle taxis cover short distances in town (40–80 THB). For the ferry to Koh Tao, Lomprayah and Songserm operate their own buses from central Chumphon hotels to the pier — book combined bus-ferry tickets online or at guesthouses for convenience.
travelTips: Book Koh Tao ferries in advance during peak season (Dec–Feb and Jul–Aug) — Lomprayah high-speed catamarans fill up fast
Rent a motorbike to explore — public songthaews only serve a few main routes and stop running by late afternoon
Try dim sum breakfast at Hor Jiah restaurant on the main street — locals queue from 6 AM and it sells out by 9 AM
The best crab restaurants are along the coastal road toward Thung Wua Laen — look for fresh tanks at the entrance
Chumphon Airport is 35 km north of town; confirm your transfer in advance as taxis are scarce at the airport
Diving season runs February–October — outside these months the Gulf of Thailand can be choppy and visibility poor
Stock up on fresh Chumphon coffee and vacuum-packed durian at the market before departing — excellent gifts
Watch the eagle migration from Ao Thung Makham cape in September–October; thousands of birds of prey pass overhead on their way south

sources.sourceName: Wikipedia — Mu Ko Chumphon National Park
